objections.objection: The scenario proposes association-run/network models but treats legal change as the main barrier while failing to specify any durable anti-reversal safeguards. This contradicts its own cited evidence that the state 'retains coercive reversal capacity' (2023 building-transfer law) and that such coercion is a real, documented risk. The plan therefore assumes (without evidence) that a simple legal-category creation will be sufficient even though the dossier shows the central state has recently and unambiguously exercised power to reallocate buildings and overturn local arrangements. In practice, association-run schools would remain exposed to unilateral asset grabs or re-centralization unless explicit entrenchment measures are specified.
objections.suggested_revision: Redesign S3 to include concrete entrenchment measures: (a) specify statutory protections for maintainer-change agreements (e.g., time-limited transfer of building-title or long-term lease with express immunity from unilateral state expropriation); (b) require multi-year, ring-fenced funding commitments for any association-run schools (3–5 year guaranteed budget lines enacted in primary law or multi-year budget law) before maintainer status is approved; (c) build mandatory contractual clauses for pilots granting judicial-reviewable appeal rights to maintainers/parents if national authorities seek reversal; and (d) include a legal feasibility appendix (draft amendments and precedent clauses) and a pilot conditioned on those protections being enacted.)

objections.objection: The scenario presents an advisory registry/threshold framework as a credible means to 'inform (not dictate)' decisions, but the evidence tag is weak and the text itself documents the core counterfactual: non-binding frameworks are regularly co-opted to legitimize pre-determined closures. The dossier therefore overstates the likely effectiveness of an advisory registry absent enforceable mechanisms. The claim that an advisory registry will produce neutral, informative outcomes is unsupported by the cited evidence and by the synthesis observation that tankerületi központs already exercise closure prerogative without binding oversight.
objections.suggested_revision: Recast S5 to distinguish two alternative designs and commit to the stronger, evidence-aligned option: (1) if advisory-only, add mandatory process safeguards (automatic independent audit before any closure citing the registry, publicly published rationales tied to registry metrics, and a 12-month mandatory consultation pause); or (2) better, legislate binding effect for key registry triggers (e.g., a closure moratorium unless registry-derived threshold tests and an independent oversight clearance are satisfied). In either alternative, specify the independent oversight body (ombudsman or auditor) with statutory powers, name the budget line that will fund the registry for at least 5 years, and include drafted legal text amending Nkt. §102(2) to create enforceable parental appeal rights triggered by registry findings.

objections.objection: S2 notes that Poland's 1999 reform was later reversed and that parental/union opposition is plausible, yet the scenario downplays how easily such structural reorganization can be politically undone unless it is legally and fiscally entrenched. It does not specify concrete entrenchment (statutory amendments, multi-year budget guarantees, collective-bargaining agreements) and therefore underestimates the risk that consolidated ISCED2 arrangements will be reversed or blocked by parental mobilization, local petitions, or union action—precisely the dynamics the scenario cites in its own evidence (Poland reversal; Budapest XVIII petition).
objections.suggested_revision: Augment S2 with an explicit entrenchment and political-mitigation package: (a) require consolidation pilots to be accompanied by a multi-year budget law clause guaranteeing transport funding and receiving-school operating subsidies for the pilot period; (b) codify teacher redeployment protections in law or in binding collective-bargaining memoranda (salary, housing support, voluntary relocation windows) to reduce union resistance; (c) build in parental-consent mechanisms (e.g., statutory consultation plus a structured appeals process and a non-binding local referendum threshold) to reduce surprise backlash; and (d) propose a legislative sunset-review (5–7 years) rather than open-ended restructuring, paired with pre-specified evaluation metrics to reduce political incentives for immediate reversal.
